The **Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland**[[g]](#cite_note-a-12) is the supreme [legislative body](/wiki/Legislative_body "Legislative body") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and may also legislate for the [Crown Dependencies](/wiki/Crown_Dependencies "Crown Dependencies") and the [British Overseas Territories](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories").[[h]](#cite_note-13)[[6]](#cite_note-14)[[7]](#cite_note-15) Since 1999, varying degrees of powers have been devolved to the national parliaments of [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), and [Wales](/wiki/Welsh_Parliament "Welsh Parliament").[[8]](#cite_note-16) Each devolved parliament has different devolved powers, with Scotland being the most powerful amongst the three devolved parliaments.[[9]](#cite_note-17) The central UK Parliament retains the power to legislate in [reserved matters](/wiki/Devolved,_reserved_and_excepted_matters "Devolved, reserved and excepted matters"), including broadcasting, defence, and currency.[[10]](#cite_note-18)

It meets at the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") in [London](/wiki/London "London"). [Parliament possesses legislative supremacy](/wiki/Parliamentary_sovereignty "Parliamentary sovereignty") and thereby holds ultimate power over all other political bodies in the United Kingdom and the Overseas Territories. While Parliament is [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral"), it has three parts: [the sovereign](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), and the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom").[[11]](#cite_note-19) The three parts acting together to legislate may be described as the [King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament").[[12]](#cite_note-20) [The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") normally acts on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional) "Advice (constitutional)") of the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom"), while the House of Lords’ powers are generally limited to delaying legislation.[[13]](#cite_note-21)

The House of Commons is the elected [lower chamber](/wiki/Lower_chamber "Lower chamber") of Parliament, with [elections](/wiki/Elections_in_the_United_Kingdom "Elections in the United Kingdom") to [650 single-member constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") held at least every five years under the [first-past-the-post system](/wiki/First-past-the-post_system "First-past-the-post system").[[14]](#cite_note-22) By [constitutional convention](/wiki/Constitutional_conventions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitutional conventions of the United Kingdom"), all [government ministers](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Crown "Minister of the Crown"), including the prime minister, are members of the House of Commons (MPs), or less commonly the House of Lords, and are thereby accountable to the respective branches of the legislature. Most [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom") ministers are from the Commons, while junior ministers can be from either house.

The House of Lords is the [upper chamber](/wiki/Upper_chamber "Upper chamber") of Parliament,[[15]](#cite_note-23) comprising two types of members. The most numerous are the [Lords Temporal](/wiki/Lords_Temporal "Lords Temporal"), consisting mainly of [life peers](/wiki/Life_peers "Life peers") appointed by the sovereign on the advice of the prime minister,[[16]](#cite_note-24) plus up to 92 [hereditary peers](/wiki/Hereditary_peer "Hereditary peer"). The less numerous [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual") consist of up to 26 bishops of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). Before the establishment of the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") in 2009, the [House of Lords performed judicial functions](/wiki/Judicial_functions_of_the_House_of_Lords "Judicial functions of the House of Lords") through the [law lords](/wiki/Law_lords "Law lords").

The Parliament of the United Kingdom is one of the oldest legislatures in the world, and is characterised by the stability of its governing institutions and its capacity to absorb change.[[17]](#cite_note-25) The [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") shaped the political systems of the nations once ruled by the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), and thus has been called the "[mother of parliaments](/wiki/Mother_of_parliaments "Mother of parliaments")".[[18]](#cite_note-26)[[i]](#cite_note-28)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

For histories of the parliaments preceding the UK Parliament, see [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"), [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland"), [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), and [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland").

For a summary, see [Parliament in the Making](/wiki/Parliament_in_the_Making#Historical_events_in_the_development_of_Parliament "Parliament in the Making").

The [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") was formed in 1707 following the ratification of the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") by [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") passed by the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") (established 1215) and the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") (c. 1235), both Acts of Union stating, "That the United Kingdom of Great Britain be represented by one and the same Parliament to be styled The Parliament of Great Britain." At the start of the 19th century, Parliament was further enlarged by [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") ratified by the Parliament of Great Britain and the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland"), which abolished the latter and added 100 Irish MPs and 32 Lords to the former to create the Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland. The [Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927](/wiki/Royal_and_Parliamentary_Titles_Act_1927 "Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927") formally amended the name to the "Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland",[[20]](#cite_note-29) five years after the secession of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"). The UK operates as a parliamentary democracy with the monarch serving as the ceremonial head of state.
[[21]](#cite_note-30)

Parliament meets in the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), the same location as the English Parliament had met, and it has continued there up to the present. The House of Lords and House of Commons sit in their respective chambers, with exception following the 1834 fire and 1941 bombing during [the Blitz](/wiki/The_Blitz "The Blitz") which destroyed large parts of the chambers. During the reconstruction the Lords sat in the Painted Chamber and White Chamber while the Commons sat in the former Lords Chamber for 17 years following the fire until 1851.[[22]](#cite_note-31)[[23]](#cite_note-32) As the Commons Chamber was destroyed following the bombing, both houses met in the Church House annexe and then the Commons met to the Lords Chamber and the Lords met in the Robing Room for 9 years until 1950.[[24]](#cite_note-33)

### Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Print_of_Houses_of_Parliament_before_1834_Fire.jpg/250px-Print_of_Houses_of_Parliament_before_1834_Fire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Print_of_Houses_of_Parliament_before_1834_Fire.jpg)

Print of the [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), before [it burnt down in 1834](/wiki/Burning_of_Parliament "Burning of Parliament")

The [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") was created on 1 January 1801, by the merger of the Kingdoms of [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") under the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"). The principle of ministerial responsibility to the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") (Commons) did not develop until the 19th century—the House of Lords was superior to the House of Commons both in theory and in practice. Members of the House of Commons (MPs) were elected in an antiquated [electoral system](/wiki/Electoral_system "Electoral system"), under which [constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies "Constituencies") of vastly different sizes existed. Thus, the borough of [Old Sarum](/wiki/Old_Sarum_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Old Sarum (UK Parliament constituency)"), with seven voters, could elect two members, as could the borough of [Dunwich](/wiki/Dunwich_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Dunwich (UK Parliament constituency)"), which had almost completely disappeared into the sea due to land erosion.

Many small constituencies, known as pocket or [rotten boroughs](/wiki/Rotten_borough "Rotten borough"), were controlled by members of the House of Lords ([peers](/wiki/Peerages_in_the_United_Kingdom "Peerages in the United Kingdom")), who could ensure the [election](/wiki/Election "Election") of their relatives or supporters. During the reforms of the 19th century, beginning with the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"), the electoral system for the House of Commons was progressively regularised. No longer dependent on the Lords for their seats, MPs grew more assertive.

The [Chartist](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism") movement gained popularity at this time, formed mostly of emerging working class people advocating peacefully for reform. The People's Charter petition garnered millions of signatures and was presented to Parliament in 1838, 1842, and 1848 with the following demands:[[25]](#cite_note-34)

* A vote for every man aged twenty-one years and above, of sound mind, and not undergoing punishment for a crime.
* The [secret ballot](/wiki/Secret_ballot "Secret ballot") to protect the elector in the exercise of his vote.
* No property qualification for [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MPs), to allow the constituencies to return the man of their choice.
* Payment of Members, enabling tradesmen, working men, or other persons of modest means to leave or interrupt their livelihood to attend to the interests of the nation.
* Equal constituencies, securing the same amount of representation for the same number of electors, instead of allowing less populous constituencies to have as much or more weight than larger ones.
* Annual parliamentary elections, thus presenting the most effectual check to bribery and intimidation, since no purse could buy a constituency under a system of [universal manhood suffrage](/wiki/Universal_manhood_suffrage "Universal manhood suffrage") in every twelve months.

The [Second Reform Act (1867)](/wiki/Reform_Act_1867 "Reform Act 1867"), and [Third Reform Act (1884)](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1884 "Representation of the People Act 1884") expanded the franchise gradually, and the adoption of all but the last of these demands came through:

* [Representation of the People Act 1918](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1918 "Representation of the People Act 1918") (gave the vote to all men over 21 as well as women over 30)
* [Ballot Act 1872](/wiki/Ballot_Act_1872 "Ballot Act 1872") (introduced secret ballots)
* [Abolish Property Qualifications Act 1858](/w/index.php?title=Abolish_Property_Qualifications_Act_1858&action=edit&redlink=1 "Abolish Property Qualifications Act 1858 (page does not exist)") (abolished property requirements for MPs)
* [Parliament Act 1911](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911") (Introduced payment for MPs, initially £400 annually)
* [Redistribution of Seats Act 1885](/wiki/Redistribution_of_Seats_Act_1885 "Redistribution of Seats Act 1885"), [Representation of the People Act 1948](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1948 "Representation of the People Act 1948"), and [Redistribution of Seats Act 1949](/wiki/House_of_Commons_(Redistribution_of_Seats)_Act_1949 "House of Commons (Redistribution of Seats) Act 1949") (made equally populated constituencies, and later abolished multi-member constituencies)

The supremacy of the British House of Commons was reaffirmed in the early 20th century. In 1909, the Commons passed the "[People's Budget](/wiki/People%27s_Budget "People's Budget")", which made numerous changes to the taxation system which were detrimental to wealthy landowners. The House of Lords, which consisted mostly of powerful landowners, rejected the Budget. On the basis of the Budget's popularity and the Lords' consequent unpopularity, the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") narrowly won two general elections in 1910.

Using the result as a mandate, the Liberal Prime Minister, [H. H. Asquith](/wiki/H._H._Asquith "H. H. Asquith"), introduced the Parliament Bill, which sought to restrict the powers of the House of Lords. (He did not reintroduce the land tax provision of the People's Budget.) When the Lords refused to pass the bill, Asquith countered with a promise extracted from the King in secret before the second general election of 1910 and requested the creation of several hundred Liberal peers, so as to erase the [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") majority in the House of Lords. In the face of such a threat, the House of Lords narrowly passed the bill.

The [Parliament Act 1911](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911"), as it became, prevented the Lords from blocking a [money bill](/wiki/Money_bill "Money bill") (a bill dealing with taxation), and allowed them to delay any other bill for a maximum of three sessions (reduced to two sessions in 1949), after which it could become law over their objections. However, regardless of the Parliament Acts of 1911 and 1949, the House of Lords has always retained the unrestricted power to veto any bill outright which attempts to extend the life of a parliament.[[26]](#cite_note-35)

### Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland")]

The [result of the 1918 general election in Ireland](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Ireland "1918 United Kingdom general election in Ireland") showed a landslide victory for the [Irish republican](/wiki/Irish_republican "Irish republican") party [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin"), who vowed in their [manifesto](/wiki/1918_Sinn_F%C3%A9in_election_manifesto "1918 Sinn Féin election manifesto") to establish an independent [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic "Irish Republic"). Accordingly, Sinn Féin MPs, though ostensibly elected to sit in the House of Commons, [refused to take their seats](/wiki/Abstentionism "Abstentionism") in Westminster, and instead assembled in 1919 to [proclaim Irish independence](/wiki/Irish_Declaration_of_Independence "Irish Declaration of Independence") and form a revolutionary [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") parliament for the independent Irish Republic, called [Dáil Éireann](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann_(Irish_Republic) "Dáil Éireann (Irish Republic)").

In 1920, in parallel to the Dáil, the [Government of Ireland Act 1920](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1920 "Government of Ireland Act 1920") created [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") parliaments of [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") and [Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%9322) "Southern Ireland (1921–22)") and reduced the representation of both parts at Westminster. The number of Northern Ireland seats was increased again after the introduction of [direct rule](/wiki/Direct_rule_over_Northern_Ireland "Direct rule over Northern Ireland") in 1973.

The Irish republicans responded by declaring the elections to these home rule Parliaments, [held on the same day in 1921](/wiki/1921_Northern_Ireland_and_Southern_Ireland_general_elections "1921 Northern Ireland and Southern Ireland general elections"), to be the basis of membership for a new Dáil Éireann. While the elections in Northern Ireland were both contested and won by Unionist parties, in Southern Ireland, all 128 candidates for the Southern Irish seats were returned unopposed. Of these, 124 were won by Sinn Féin and four by independent Unionists representing [Dublin University](/wiki/Dublin_University_(constituency) "Dublin University (constituency)") (Trinity College).[[27]](#cite_note-36) Since only four MPs sat in the home rule Southern Irish parliament, with the remaining 124 being in the Republic's [Second Dáil](/wiki/Second_D%C3%A1il "Second Dáil"), the home rule parliament was adjourned *[sine die](/wiki/Sine_die "Sine die")* without ever having operated.

[![Victoria Tower In London.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Victoria_Tower%2C_London..jpg/250px-Victoria_Tower%2C_London..jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_Tower,_London..jpg)

Victoria Tower in London

In 1922, pursuant to the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty"), the revolutionary Irish Republic was replaced by the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"), recognised by the United Kingdom as a separate state (and thus, no longer represented in the Westminster Parliament), while Northern Ireland would remain British, and in 1927, parliament was renamed the Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

The [Reform Act 1928](/wiki/Reform_Act_1928 "Reform Act 1928") gave [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") with a [voting age](/wiki/Voting_age "Voting age") of 21, and the Reform Act 1969 lowered the voting age to 18, making the United Kingdom the first major democratic country to do so.

Further reforms to the House of Lords were made in the 20th century. The [Life Peerages Act 1958](/wiki/Life_Peerages_Act_1958 "Life Peerages Act 1958") authorised the regular creation of [life peerage](/wiki/Life_peerage "Life peerage") dignities. By the 1960s, the regular creation of hereditary peerage dignities had ceased; thereafter, almost all new peers were life peers only.

The [House of Lords Act 1999](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Act_1999 "House of Lords Act 1999") removed the automatic right of hereditary peers to sit in the House of Lords, although it made an exception for 92 of them to be elected to life-terms by the other hereditary peers, with by-elections upon their death. The House of Lords is now a chamber that is subordinate to the House of Commons. Additionally, the [Constitutional Reform Act 2005](/wiki/Constitutional_Reform_Act_2005 "Constitutional Reform Act 2005") led to abolition of the [judicial functions of the House of Lords](/wiki/Judicial_functions_of_the_House_of_Lords "Judicial functions of the House of Lords") with the creation of the new [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") in October 2009.

## Composition and powers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Composition and powers")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Special:EditPage/Parliament of the United Kingdom") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(October 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Under the [UK's constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitution of the United Kingdom"), Parliament is the supreme legislative body of the state. Whilst the [privy council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)") can also issue legislation through [orders-in-council](/wiki/Orders-in-council "Orders-in-council"), this power may be limited by Parliament, like all other exercises of the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative_in_the_United_Kingdom "Royal prerogative in the United Kingdom").

The legislative authority, the [King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament"), has three separate elements: the Monarch, the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), and the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). As a result, a bill must be passed by both houses (or just the House of Commons under the *[Parliament Act 1911](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911")*) and receive [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") for it to become law.

Executive powers (including those granted by legislation or forming part of the prerogative) are not formally exercised by Parliament itself. However, these powers are in practice exercised on the advice of [government ministers](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom"), who must be drawn from and be accountable to the Parliament.

### Monarch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Monarch")]

Main article: [King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament")

Whilst the monarch is a constitutive element of parliament, they do not debate bills or otherwise contribute to political debate. Their [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") is required for a bill to become law; however, this has not been refused since 1708 and is largely a formality. Assent is granted by the monarch (or [counsellors of state](/wiki/Counsellor_of_State "Counsellor of State")) signing letters patent (affixed with the [Great Seal of the Realm](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_the_Realm "Great Seal of the Realm")) prepared by the [clerk of the Crown](/wiki/Clerk_of_the_Crown_in_Chancery "Clerk of the Crown in Chancery") listing all bills passed by the houses of Parliament up to a certain date. To be complete, Parliament must be notified that assent has been given. Assent may also be granted in person by the monarch in the House of Lords; however, this has not happened since 1854. Alternatively, assent may be granted by [royal commission](/wiki/Lords_Commissioners "Lords Commissioners"), as commonly occurred before 1967 but now only occurs when bills are assented to before prorogation.[[28]](#cite_note-37)

### House of Lords

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: House of Lords")]

Main article: [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords")

The House of Lords is known formally as "The Right Honourable The Lords Spiritual and Temporal in Parliament Assembled", the Lords Spiritual being bishops of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and the Lords Temporal being [Peers of the Realm](/wiki/Peerage "Peerage"). The Lords Spiritual and Lords Temporal are considered separate "[estates](/wiki/Estates_of_the_realm "Estates of the realm")", but they sit, debate, and vote together.

The [Parliament Acts 1911 and 1949](/wiki/Parliament_Acts_1911_and_1949 "Parliament Acts 1911 and 1949") significantly reduced the legislative powers of the House of Lords relative to the House of Commons. Whilst the Lords debates and votes on all bills (except [money bills](/wiki/Money_bills "Money bills")), their refusal to pass a bill may only delay its passage for a maximum of two parliamentary sessions over a year. After this, the bill may receive royal assent and become law without the Lords' consent.

Like in the House of Commons, the House of Lords may scrutinise governments through asking questions to government ministers that sit in the Lords and through the operation of a small number of [select committees](/wiki/Select_committee_(United_Kingdom) "Select committee (United Kingdom)").

The Lords used to also exercise judicial power and acted as the [UK's supreme legislative court](/wiki/Judicial_functions_of_the_House_of_Lords "Judicial functions of the House of Lords"). Appeals were not heard by the whole body, but a committee of senior judges that were appointed to the Lords to act for this purpose. This power was lost when it was transferred to the newly created [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") in 2009.

From time to time, and especially recently, arguments have been made suggesting the House of Lords is obsolete or unnecessary.[[29]](#cite_note-38) In 2023, a commission led by former Prime Minister [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") stated that the existence of the House of Lords was "indefensible" and recommended that the Lords in its current form be abolished and replaced by an "Assembly of the Nations and Regions".[[30]](#cite_note-39)

#### Lords Spiritual

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Lords Spiritual")]

Main article: [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual")

The Lords Spiritual of the Lords' currently consists of the archbishops of [Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") and [York](/wiki/Archbishop_of_York "Archbishop of York"), the bishops of [London](/wiki/Bishop_of_London "Bishop of London"), [Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") and [Winchester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Winchester "Bishop of Winchester") (who sit by right regardless of seniority) and 21 other diocesan bishops of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), ranked in order of [consecration](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration"), subject to women being preferred if one is eligible from 2015 to 2025 under the *[Lords Spiritual (Women) Act 2015](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual_(Women)_Act_2015 "Lords Spiritual (Women) Act 2015").* Formerly, the Lords Spiritual included all of the senior clergymen of the Church of England—archbishops, bishops, abbots and mitred priors.

#### Lords Temporal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Lords Temporal")]

Main article: [Lords Temporal](/wiki/Lords_Temporal "Lords Temporal")

The Lords Temporal consists of [92 hereditary peers](/wiki/List_of_excepted_hereditary_peers "List of excepted hereditary peers") and all life peers appointed under the [Life Peerages Act 1958](/wiki/Life_Peerages_Act_1958 "Life Peerages Act 1958") (currently numbering around 700).

Two hereditary peers sit ex officio by virtue of being the [Earl Marshal](/wiki/Earl_Marshal "Earl Marshal") or the [Lord Great Chamberlain](/wiki/Lord_Great_Chamberlain "Lord Great Chamberlain"). Each of the other 90 are elected for life upon a seat becoming vacant. 15 members are elected by the whole House whilst the other 75 are elected by all the [hereditary peers](/wiki/Hereditary_peer "Hereditary peer"), including those not sitting in the Lords. Currently, a standing rule divides these seats between the parties with a replacement peer from one party subject to an election only by peers of that party. Formerly, all hereditary peers were members of the Lords, until the passage of the [House of Lords Act 1999](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Act_1999 "House of Lords Act 1999") limited their numbers to 92.

Life peers are appointed by the monarch, on the advice of the prime minister. Typically, these are members of the party of the prime minister, however some peers from other parties are also generally appointed.

### House of Commons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: House of Commons")]

Main article: [House of Commons of the United Kingdom](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom")

As of 2019, the House consists of 650 members; this total includes the Speaker, who by convention renounces partisan affiliation and does not take part in debates or votes, as well as three Deputy Speakers, who also do not participate in debates or votes but formally retain their party membership. Each Member of Parliament (MP) is chosen by a single constituency by the [First-Past-the-Post](/wiki/First_Past_the_Post_electoral_system "First Past the Post electoral system") electoral system. There are 650 constituencies in the United Kingdom, each made up of an average of 65,925 voters. The First-Past-the-Post system means that every constituency elects one MP each. Each voter assigns one vote for one candidate, and the candidate with the most votes in each constituency is elected as MP to represent their constituency. Members sit for a maximum of five years, although elections are generally called before that maximum limit is reached.

A party needs to win 326 constituencies (known as "seats") to win a majority in the House of Commons. If no party achieves a majority, then a situation of no overall control occurs – commonly known as a "Hung Parliament". In case of a Hung Parliament, the party with the most seats has the opportunity to form a coalition with other parties, so their combined seat tally extends past the 326-seat majority.

The House of Commons is the most powerful of the components of Parliament, particularly due to its sole right to determine taxation and the supply of money to the government. Additionally, the prime minister and leader of the government sits in the House, having acquiring this position by virtue of having the confidence of the other members. This also means that the House is also the primary location in which the government faces scrutiny, as expressed through [Question Time](/wiki/Prime_Minister%27s_Questions "Prime Minister's Questions") and the work of various [select committees](/wiki/Select_committee_(United_Kingdom) "Select committee (United Kingdom)").

## State Opening of Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: State Opening of Parliament")]

Main article: [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament")

The State Opening of Parliament is an annual event that marks the commencement of a session of the Parliament of the United Kingdom. It is held in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") Chamber. Before 2012, it took place in November or December,[[31]](#cite_note-40) or, in a general election year, when the new Parliament first assembled. From 2012 onwards, the ceremony has taken place in May or June.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/John_Hampden_portrait.jpg/250px-John_Hampden_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Hampden_portrait.jpg)

Leading 17th-century Parliamentarian [John Hampden](/wiki/John_Hampden "John Hampden") is one of the [Five Members](/wiki/Five_Members "Five Members") annually commemorated.

Upon the signal of the Monarch, the [Lord Great Chamberlain](/wiki/Lord_Great_Chamberlain "Lord Great Chamberlain") raises their wand of office to signal to [Black Rod](/wiki/Black_Rod "Black Rod"), who is charged with summoning the House of Commons and has been waiting in the Commons lobby. Black Rod turns and, under the escort of the Door-keeper of the House of Lords and an [inspector of police](/wiki/Inspector#United_Kingdom "Inspector"), approaches the doors to the Chamber of the Commons. In 1642, King [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") stormed into the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_England "House of Commons of England") in an unsuccessful attempt to arrest the [Five Members](/wiki/Five_Members "Five Members"), who included the celebrated English patriot and leading Parliamentarian [John Hampden](/wiki/John_Hampden "John Hampden"). This action sparked the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War").[[32]](#cite_note-41)[[33]](#cite_note-42) The wars established the constitutional rights of Parliament, a concept legally established in the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") in 1688 and the subsequent [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689"). Since then, no British monarch has entered the House of Commons when it is in session.[[34]](#cite_note-State_opening-43) On Black Rod's approach, the doors are slammed shut against them, symbolising the rights of parliament and its independence from the monarch.[[34]](#cite_note-State_opening-43) They then strike, with the end of their ceremonial staff (the Black Rod), three times on the closed doors of the Commons Chamber. They are then admitted, and announce the command of the monarch for the attendance of the Commons.[[34]](#cite_note-State_opening-43)

The monarch reads a speech, known as the [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_Throne "Speech from the Throne"), which is prepared by the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") and the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom"), outlining the Government's agenda for the coming year. The speech reflects the legislative agenda for which the Government intends to seek the agreement of both Houses of Parliament.

After the monarch leaves, each Chamber proceeds to the consideration of an "Address in Reply to His Majesty's Gracious Speech." But, first, each House considers a bill *[pro forma](/wiki/Pro_forma "Pro forma")* to symbolise their right to deliberate independently of the monarch. In the House of Lords, the bill is called the [Select Vestries Bill](/wiki/Select_Vestries_Bill "Select Vestries Bill"), while the Commons equivalent is the [Outlawries Bill](/wiki/Outlawries_Bill "Outlawries Bill"). The Bills are considered for the sake of form only, and do not make any actual progress.

## Legislative procedure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legislative procedure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/John_Singleton_Copley_-_Henry_Addington%2C_First_Viscount_Sidmouth_-_26-1929_-_Saint_Louis_Art_Museum.jpg/250px-John_Singleton_Copley_-_Henry_Addington%2C_First_Viscount_Sidmouth_-_26-1929_-_Saint_Louis_Art_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Singleton_Copley_-_Henry_Addington,_First_Viscount_Sidmouth_-_26-1929_-_Saint_Louis_Art_Museum.jpg)

[Henry Addington](/wiki/Henry_Addington "Henry Addington") in state robes. Portrait by [John Singleton Copley](/wiki/John_Singleton_Copley "John Singleton Copley"), 1797–98.

:   *See also the [stages of a bill](/wiki/Acts_of_Parliament_in_the_United_Kingdom#Stages_of_a_bill "Acts of Parliament in the United Kingdom") section in [Acts of Parliament in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Acts_of_Parliament_in_the_United_Kingdom "Acts of Parliament in the United Kingdom").*

Both houses of the British Parliament are presided over by a speaker, the [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)") for the Commons and the [Lord Speaker](/wiki/Lord_Speaker "Lord Speaker") in the House of Lords.

For the Commons, the approval of the Sovereign is required before the election of the Speaker becomes valid, but it is, by modern convention, always granted. The Speaker's place may be taken by the [Chairman of Ways and Means](/wiki/Chairman_of_Ways_and_Means "Chairman of Ways and Means"), the First Deputy Chairman, or the Second Deputy Chairman. (The titles of those three officials refer to the Committee of Ways and Means, a body which no longer exists.)

Prior to July 2006, the House of Lords was presided over by a [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") (a Cabinet member), whose influence as Speaker was very limited (whilst the powers belonging to the Speaker of the House of Commons are vast). However, as part of the [Constitutional Reform Act 2005](/wiki/Constitutional_Reform_Act_2005 "Constitutional Reform Act 2005"), the position of Speaker of the House of Lords (as it is termed in the Act) was separated from the office of Lord Chancellor (the office which has control over the judiciary as a whole), though the Lords remain largely self-governing. Decisions on points of order and on the disciplining of unruly members are made by the whole body, but by the Speaker alone in the Lower House. Speeches in the House of Lords are addressed to the House as a whole (using the words "My Lords"), but those in the House of Commons are addressed to the Speaker alone (using "Mr Speaker" or "Madam Speaker"). Speeches may be made to [both Houses simultaneously](/wiki/List_of_people_who_have_addressed_both_Houses_of_the_United_Kingdom_Parliament "List of people who have addressed both Houses of the United Kingdom Parliament").

Both Houses may decide questions by [voice vote](/wiki/Voice_vote "Voice vote"); members shout out "Aye!" and "No!" in the Commons—or "Content!" and "Not-Content!" in the Lords—and the presiding officer declares the result. The pronouncement of either Speaker may be challenged, and a recorded vote (known as a [division](/wiki/Division_(vote) "Division (vote)")) demanded. (The Speaker of the House of Commons may choose to overrule a frivolous request for a division, but the Lord Speaker does not have that power.) In each House, a division requires members to file into one of the two lobbies alongside the Chamber; their names are recorded by clerks, and their votes are counted as they exit the lobbies to re-enter the Chamber. The Speaker of the House of Commons is expected to be non-partisan, and does not cast a vote except in the case of a tie; the Lord Speaker, however, votes along with the other Lords. [Speaker Denison's rule](/wiki/Speaker_Denison%27s_rule "Speaker Denison's rule") is a convention which concerns how the Speaker should vote should he be required to break a tie.

Both Houses normally conduct their business in public, and there are galleries where visitors may sit.

## Duration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Duration")]

Originally there was no fixed limit on the length of a Parliament, but the [Triennial Act 1694](/wiki/Triennial_Act_1694 "Triennial Act 1694") set the maximum duration at three years. As the frequent elections were deemed inconvenient, the [Septennial Act 1715](/wiki/Septennial_Act_1715 "Septennial Act 1715") extended the maximum to seven years, but the [Parliament Act 1911](/wiki/Parliament_Act_1911 "Parliament Act 1911") reduced it to five. During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the term was temporarily extended to ten years by Acts of Parliament. Since the end of the war the maximum has remained five years. Modern Parliaments, however, rarely continued for the maximum duration; normally, they were dissolved earlier. For instance, the [52nd](/wiki/Fifty-Second_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Fifty-Second Parliament of the United Kingdom"), which assembled in 1997, was dissolved after four years. The Septennial Act was repealed by the [Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011](/wiki/Fixed-term_Parliaments_Act_2011 "Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011"), which established a presumption that a Parliament will last for five years, unless two thirds of the House of Commons votes for an early general election, or the government loses the confidence of the House. This was repealed by the [Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022](/wiki/Dissolution_and_Calling_of_Parliament_Act_2022 "Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022"), which restored the ability for the government to call an early election while keeping five year terms.
